subject: Carpenter v. United States (2018) and the warrant requirement for historical cell-site location information
central_question: Your phone records where you are every few minutes and hands it to your carrier — so when the police want that map of your life, do they need a warrant?
viewer_promise: By the end, the viewer understands how the Supreme Court in 2018 decided that a record you cannot avoid creating — your constant location — is too revealing to lose its privacy automatically, bringing the Fourth Amendment into the smartphone age.
surprise: We assume anything you share with a company loses its privacy; Carpenter held that your location trail is different — you never really volunteer it, and it maps your whole life.
stakes: Whether the government can obtain a detailed, months-long map of your movements from your phone company without a warrant.
target_audience: English-speaking US general audience interested in privacy, surveillance, technology and law
demand_evidence: Peak relevance: data brokers, location selling, government surveillance and AI make phone-tracking a live, high-search anxiety.
Recent, recognizable landmark; the climax of the EP6-8 privacy trilogy.
competition: Most coverage is legal-news or tech-policy; few center the neutral 'why location is different in kind' angle and land it on the viewer's own phone.
differentiation: Present-tense, self-relevant payoff (the device in your hand); primary-source citations; series capstone that pays off Terry (the body) and Riley (the phone's contents).
research_feasibility: High: opinion is public domain (585 U.S. 296 / 138 S.Ct. 2206); slip opinion, Cornell LII, Lawfare and DOJ records available.
visual_feasibility: High: animated map of a phone's location trail building over 127 days, contrast with a single dialed-number list, timeline to 2018.
risk_notes: Decided landmark case (2018); opinion is public domain - advertiser-friendly.
Timothy Carpenter is a convicted person on the public record: describe neutrally and factually; no editorializing on guilt beyond the record; no real-person likeness in AI visuals (invariant 11).
Surveillance topic: keep neutral and educational, not alarmist.
Generated visuals are illustration/reconstruction, never presented as authentic footage.
kill_conditions: Cannot source the holding and key facts to the primary opinion plus one independent reference.
